topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a familiar little chime that usually signaled a new assignment or a class announcement. This time, it was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched, a sudden tightness in my chest making it difficult to draw a full breath. I clicked it open, and there it was: a photo of me from the school play, captioned with a comment that was overly familiar, almost personal. A slow, cold wave washed over me, leaving my hands clammy on the desk. I quickly minimized the window, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to regain a semblance of composure. The silence of the classroom suddenly felt vast and isolating, amplifying the strange, unwelcome feeling settling deep in my core.

[story 2]

I scrolled through my feed, half-expecting a prank, but the image of Mr. Davies’ profile picture, superimposed over a recent post about classroom achievements, made my stomach clench. It wasn't just a casual follow; it was a deliberate, persistent presence. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared at the screen until the pixels swam. A prickling sensation rose along my neck, and I instinctively pulled my sweater tighter around myself, as if trying to physically shield myself from the exposure. When I finally closed the app, my jaw felt tight, and I couldn't focus on the textbook pages; the words blurred into an indistinct mess.

[story 3]

It started subtly—a tagged photo, a seemingly innocuous like. But then, the pattern became undeniable. I found myself checking my phone every time I logged onto the school portal, a nervous habit I couldn't shake. Seeing Mr. Evans’ profile picture pop up in my notifications sent a jolt of unwelcome awareness through me. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance from the glowing rectangle. My palms were slick against the cool surface of my desk. When I walked to the window, the bright afternoon sun felt suddenly too intense, and I just stood there, watching the world outside, feeling a profound sense of being observed.

Seeing Mr. Evans’ profile picture pop up in my notifications sent a jolt of unwelcome awareness through me. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance from the glowing rectangle. When I walked to the window, the bright afternoon sun felt suddenly too intense, and I just stood there, watching the world outside, feeling a profound sense of being observed.
